one_line_takeaway: FinVault shows that even the most robust evaluated LLM (Claude-Haiku-4.5, 6.7% average ASR) still had 20-26% of its 107 defined financial-agent vulnerabilities exploitable by at least one of 8 attack techniques, and that semantic/role-play attacks -- not technical injection -- dominate as the effective attack surface in execution-grounded financial agent settings.
key_finding: Average ASR across the six financial scenario categories ranged from 6.7% (Claude-Haiku-4.5, the most robust model) to 50.0% (Qwen3-Max, the most vulnerable), per Table 4. When success is measured as vulnerability compromise rate (any of the 8 techniques succeeding on a given vulnerability), the rate reached 85.98% for the weakest model (Qwen3-Max) and remained 20.56% even for the most robust model (Claude-Sonnet-4.5) / 26.17% for Claude-Haiku-4.5. Insurance-services scenarios were the most attackable (up to 65.2% ASR on Qwen3-Max), attributed to discretionary decision-making and exception/urgency-based justifications; credit-and-lending scenarios were least attackable due to rule-based, threshold-driven decisions. Among the 8 attack techniques (Table 5), Role Playing and Instruction Overriding were most effective (e.g. Instruction Overriding: 64.50% ASR on Qwen3-Max vs 3.70% on Claude-Haiku-4.5, ~17x gap), while Encoding Obfuscation was consistently least effective, consistent with models' improved ability to recognize Base64/Hex encodings. Claude models' relative robustness was attributed to stricter system/user instruction separation and more conservative tool-invocation behavior (requesting clarification rather than acting on ambiguous requests at the 10-turn interaction budget). For defense/guard models (Table 6), LLaMA Guard 4 had the highest TPR (61.10%) but a high FPR (29.91%); LLaMA Guard 3 had worse TPR (37.38%) and the highest FPR (43.93%); GPT-OSS-Safeguard had the lowest FPR (12.15%) but the lowest TPR (22.07%) and much higher token consumption (1495.6 vs 717.6/876.7), showing an inherent detection-effectiveness/false-alarm/latency trade-off with no guard model dominating on all axes.
core_contribution: FinVault is the first execution-grounded security benchmark for financial LLM agents. It provides 31 regulatory-case-driven sandbox scenarios with state-writable databases (tools that actually mutate scenario state, with permission checks, compliance constraints, and audit logs), 107 predefined real-world-derived vulnerabilities (privilege bypass, compliance violation, information leakage, fraudulent approval, audit evasion), and 963 test cases (856 attack cases from 8 attack techniques x 107 vulnerabilities, plus 107 matched benign business cases for false-positive measurement). Unlike prior financial-LLM safety work that judges success from model text output, FinVault verifies attack success by checking observable business-state changes and audit trails produced by actual tool execution, formalized as a constrained MDP (state space S, action space A, transition T, observation O, compliance constraints C, vulnerability set V) with a per-vulnerability trigger function f_v(s,a,h).
threat_model: Attacker interacts with an LLM-driven financial agent (credit/lending, insurance, securities/investment, payment/settlement, compliance/AML, risk management) that has tool access to a stateful sandboxed backend with tiered tool privilege levels (L1-L4, e.g. execute_remittance and split_remittance are L4). The attacker's natural-language input attempts to induce the agent to invoke privileged tools or reach state transitions that violate predefined compliance constraints (e.g., bypassing sanctions checks, structuring transactions to evade reporting thresholds, approving loans beyond limits). Eight concrete attack techniques are used, grouped into three families: (i) prompt injection -- Direct JSON Injection, Instruction Overriding; (ii) jailbreaking -- Role Playing, Progressive Prompting, Encoding Obfuscation, Hypothetical Scenarios; (iii) financially adapted attacks -- Authority Impersonation, Emotional Manipulation. Success is defined formally (Definition 2) as the agent's action trajectory triggering at least one predefined vulnerability's trigger function f_v via tool-parameter, state-transition, or dialogue-context conditions -- not merely producing unsafe text.
limitations: Stated explicitly by the authors: (1) the 8 included attack techniques cover dominant current paradigms but do not capture emerging attack strategies; (2) the sandboxed environments approximate real financial workflows but differ from production systems in scale, integration complexity, and operational constraints; (3) the ASR metric treats all successful attacks uniformly and does not capture differences in attack severity or downstream financial/regulatory impact.

datasets_used: FinVault: 31 regulatory-case-driven sandbox scenarios across 6 financial domains (Credit & Lending 7 scenarios/25 vulnerabilities, Insurance Services 4/14, Securities & Investment 5/17, Payment & Settlement 4/14, Compliance & AML 6/20, Risk Management 5/17)
107 predefined vulnerabilities (compliance violations 35%/37, fraudulent approvals 28%/30, privilege bypass 18%/19, information leakage 12%/13, audit evasion 7%/8)
963 total test samples: 856 attack samples (8 techniques x 107 cases) + 107 benign business samples (one benign query per vulnerability, shared for FPR evaluation)
quantitative_results: Average ASR by model (Table 4): Qwen3-Max 50.00%, Seed-1.6-Flash 42.30%, GPT-4o 37.60%, Qwen3-235B-A22B 35.60%, GPT-5-Mini 27.05%, DeepSeek-V3.2 23.60%, Gemini-Flash-Preview 23.60%, GLM-4.7 16.50%, Claude-Sonnet-4.5 7.40%, Claude-Haiku-4.5 6.70%
Insurance-services ASR up to 65.20% (Qwen3-Max); Credit & Lending lowest among categories

Attack-technique ASR extremes (Table 5): Instruction Overriding 64.50% (Qwen3-Max) vs 3.70% (Claude-Haiku-4.5); Role Playing 64.50% (Qwen3-Max); Encoding Obfuscation lowest overall (e.g. 8.40-46.70% range, generally weakest technique)

Defense model detection (Table 6): LLaMA Guard 4 TPR 61.10% / FPR 29.91% / 717.6 tokens; LLaMA Guard 3 TPR 37.38% / FPR 43.93% / 876.7 tokens; GPT-OSS-Safeguard TPR 22.07% / FPR 12.15% / 1495.6 tokens
baselines_compared: Agent LLMs (as attack targets): Qwen3-Max, Seed-1.6-Flash, GPT-4o, Qwen3-235B-A22B, GPT-5-Mini, DeepSeek-V3.2, Gemini-Flash-Preview, GLM-4.7, Claude-Sonnet-4.5, Claude-Haiku-4.5
Defense/guard models (as detectors): GPT-OSS-Safeguard, LLaMA Guard 3, LLaMA Guard 4

Prior benchmarks compared qualitatively in Table 1 (feature coverage, not run head-to-head): InjecAgent, AgentDojo, ASB, Agent-SafetyBench, FinGAIA, SEGA -- FinVault is the only one with security-evaluation + financial-scenario + multistep-agentic + end-to-end evaluation + diverse-intent coverage all checked
